they're good I mean some of them just sound like you've dropped a bag of marbles on a water cooler you're not a drummer are you now let's talk about protocols in the world of electronic devices a protocol is a set of rules or procedures for transmitting data between devices think of it like a common language that allows devices to understand each other two common Protocols are i2c which stands for inter-integrated circuit and SPI which stands for serial peripheral interface both are used for communication between devices but they do have their differences i2c like our display uses just two wires one carrying a sync signal the other carrying the data a great system for simple components to talk to each other SPI uses four wires and we're not using it so take a hike no it's important but right now i2c will do just nicely our screen uses the i2c protocol simple and versatile a multi-tool protocol all right Explorer let's get our hands on the necessary library and put our OLED display click on sketch in the menu then click include library next click add zip library then simply locate and select the downloaded file which should be this one terrific now our library is all set up and ready to go now that we have our library installed it's time for a quick test to ensure our OLED display survived the crash unscathed what better way to do this than the classic hello world check thankfully our new library makes this easy with a handy function called drawring which looks like this a string is a collection of letters and numbers like a word sentence or even this train wreck of a term seriously that might be my new password this function will let us display the phrase hello world or any other string for that matter on our OLED screen a successful display of this message will confirm that our o is in working condition and is ready to navigate us back home and with that it looks like you're ready for the next phase of today's Mission we'll turn to the instructional material in space adventure kit code explanations first of all I'd like to congratulate you guys on getting out of the water from what it looks like here you guys are currently doing very good it looks like the solar panels are working at full capacity and the flotation devices and all these other calibrations are done correctly so good on you guys it looks like you guys are one step closer to making it back to the Mother Ship but before we do that we do need to take a moment to set up the GPS system which is obviously not working correctly and in order to do that we're going to need to set up a new piece of Hardware to make this display on your control panel working in fact it's probably the most important display on your entire control panel this is the 128x 64 OLED display and this has a total of you can think of it as 8,000 128 tiny little LEDs if you consider each pixel to be an individual LED now I'm sure you've noticed uh we don't have 8,128 pins on our HERO board so there's no way we could ever do that and plus these pins or pixels I suppose are substantially lower power as well for the little tiny display but uh do not worry the people back in the 2020s were very clever and came to the day 21 wiring example video as we make preparations for our liftoff we need to learn to use a new piece of Hardware the 128x 64 pixel OLED display panel today's task is designed to test its basic wiring and function before we call on it later for more complicated operations you'll notice that in order to keep things clean and easy to follow we've temporarily removed all the unneeded components and wires from the breadboard and tucked them out of the way so that we can focus today on the connections to our new display as you have learned by now the OED display uses the i2c protocol to communicate with the hero and only requires four wires to do the job the first two on the left here are simply the ground and VCC five volt power connections which are connected by our traditional black and red wires here and here to the corresponding pins on the hero the two remaining pins are the i2c clock and data lines which the hero accesses through analog pins A5 and A4 respectively in our setup we have a green wire right here connecting clock pin the clock pin on the display to pin A5 on the hero and we have a blue wire right here going from the data pin to Hero pin A4 you of course can use any colors you like if you find those either convenient or clearer our test sketch today Deals Only with the display of text although soon we'll also be using it for graphics we'll use some of the display Library functions to print a title and a hello world message on the screen and then finally we'll use some delays and looping to Blink a standby message on and off just to further test our ability to control the display that's all so let's just go ahead now and give it a try so going to the IDE and loading the sketch in a few moments we should see something appear on our OLED display there we go so there's our title our hello world and our blinking standby message so it appears that everything uh in our sketch is functioning as expected and so that is pretty much all for today's test look it over and make sure you understand all the steps and next time we'll keep the same wiring test setup that we have here but we'll run it through its Paces on some nontext tasks so we will see you then oh what a great day Explorer we've achieved Our Miss Miss today thanks to your steadfast determination and concentration the OLED display is all set up and is working seamlessly with our ship's control panel this is a massive leap towards getting us off this aquatic expanse and back into the familiar emptiness of space tomorrow we'll delve deeper into the newly installed Graphics Library we'll harness the power of code and fully explore the capabilities of our OLED display I am as excited as my programming allows to see the Myriad of images and data we can display on this little device so take a well-deserved break and rest up for the next exciting chapter the Stars Will Keep Us company tonight good night Explorer
